title: Setting Up Your AI Workflow
intro: Before you start vibe coding, you need a solid workflow. Just like save slots in a video game let you reload when a boss fight goes wrong, Git lets you undo bad AI-generated code. Let's set up a project with version control so you can safely experiment -- accepting what works and reverting what doesn't.

teach.title: Version Control Is Your Safety Net
teach.body: Imagine painting a picture but being able to press 'undo' at any point and go back to how it looked five minutes ago. Git gives you that for code. When you're experimenting with AI-generated code, Git lets you try things out and revert if they don't work.

teach.title: Git Is Like Save Slots in a Video Game
teach.body: In a tough video game, you save before every boss fight so you can reload if things go wrong. Git works the same way for your code. Before letting AI make big changes, you save your progress with a commit. If the AI-generated code breaks everything, you just reload your last save. No stress, no lost work.

teach.title: The Safe AI Coding Workflow
teach.body:
Before using AI to write code, set up this workflow:

1. **Initialize Git** so every change is tracked
2. **Commit your starting point** as a clean baseline
3. **Create a branch** for AI-generated code
4. **Commit in small chunks** so you can revert individual changes

This way, you can always get back to working code if AI gives you something broken.

teach.title: Setting Up Your Safety Net
teach.code:
git init
git add .
git commit -m "Initial project setup"
git checkout -b ai-feature
teach.annotation: These four commands create a Git repo, save your current code, and create a separate branch for AI experiments. If things go wrong, you can switch back to your clean main branch.

teach.body: A solid workflow means you can experiment fearlessly with AI. The faster you iterate without breaking things, the more apps you can build and ship -- and every shipped app is a potential income stream.
Always use Git when working with AI-generated code. Commit before and after AI changes so you can review diffs and revert anything that doesn't work.

steps.instruction: Initialize a Git repository so you can track changes and revert bad AI-generated code easily.
steps.hint: Use 'git init' to start tracking changes in this folder.

steps.instruction: Make an initial commit with your existing files so you have a clean starting point before adding AI code.
steps.hint: Use 'git add .' to stage all files, then you'll commit them.

steps.instruction: Now commit these files with a clear message.
steps.output:
[main (root-commit) f1a2b3c] Initial project setup
 2 files changed, 45 insertions(+)
 create mode 100644 index.js
 create mode 100644 package.json
steps.hint: Use 'git commit -m' followed by a message in quotes.

steps.instruction: Create a new branch for AI-generated code. This way you can easily compare the AI's changes to your original code and revert if needed.
acceptAlternatives: git branch ai-feature && git checkout ai-feature
steps.hint: Use 'git checkout -b' followed by the branch name 'ai-feature'.

exercise.completionMessage: You now have a safe AI coding workflow. Work on a feature branch, commit AI-generated code in small chunks, review each change, and merge only what works. If AI generates something broken, you can always reset to your last good commit.
